name: Young FMT reversed aged mouse gut, eye, and brain inflammatory changes
summary: Young-to-aged mouse FMT supports a causal role for aged microbiota in gut-brain and gut-retina inflammatory aging phenotypes.
statement: In young, old, and aged mice, FMT successfully transferred age-associated microbiota profiles; aged donor microbiota accelerated CNS and retinal inflammatory readouts in young mice, while young donor microbiota reversed detrimental gut barrier, brain, and retinal inflammatory changes in aged mice.
population_or_model: Young, old, and aged mice with reciprocal microbiota transfer
time_horizon: Post-FMT tissue, microbiome, metabolomic, and behavioral assessment
quantitative_note: The abstract reports successful transfer of microbiota profiles and reversal of detrimental effects by young donor microbiota, but does not provide numeric effect sizes.
caveats: The strongest endpoints are inflammatory, barrier, and tissue-marker readouts rather than lifespan.
Mouse gut-retina and gut-brain effects may not translate to human aging or neurodegenerative disease prevention.
Bidirectional transfer shows both potential benefit and risk of transferring age-associated detrimental phenotypes.
